headline: Semis Crater as Healthcare Leads; Geopolitical Crosscurrents Set the Tone for the Holiday-Shortened Week
body_html:
Markets closed Friday's session on a split note, with 109 of 198 names in our universe finishing higher even as a savage rotation punished chip-related names. The semiconductor sector shed 6.5% on the day — the worst reading across all eight tracked sectors — dragging memory, packaging, test-equipment, and capital-goods names sharply lower. Sandisk tumbled 14.1%, Teradyne fell 13.6%, Amkor dropped 12.9%, and KLA Corp lost 11.5%, signalling that the sector's recent momentum has encountered serious selling pressure.

The damage was concentrated: Healthcare surged 3.2% to lead all sectors, with Intuitive Surgical climbing 5.9% and Roche advancing 5.4%. Financial-data names were the surprise stars — Equifax jumped 6.1%, S&P Global added 6.0%, and Fair Isaac rose 5.3%, collectively pointing to renewed appetite for data-driven business models. Consumer names gained 1.9% and homebuilders edged up 1.0%, while Industrials slipped 0.7% and Materials fell 1.2%, with Corning off 10.8% the sharpest casualty in the latter group.

The week ahead arrives under the shadow of the NATO Ankara summit, ongoing Middle East tensions following the death of Supreme Leader Khamenei, and jittery energy markets where Gulf crude exports jumped in June on record UAE flows. Traders will be watching headline risk carefully: any escalation in the Strait of Hormuz — where a missile strike on a container vessel is already raising insurance and supply-chain alarms — could quickly reprice energy and materials. With U.S. markets closed today for Independence Day, positioning into a truncated week will likely amplify any early moves.

briefs.headline: Chip Selloff Echoes Across the Supply Chain
briefs.body: Semiconductor-adjacent names suffered one of the sharpest single-session declines of the year, with the sector index down 6.5%. Losses cascaded from memory and packaging into test equipment and materials, suggesting the selloff was broad-based rather than stock-specific. Investors will be watching whether weekend developments spark further unwinding when full trading resumes.

briefs.headline: Financial-Data Names Stage Rare Unified Rally
briefs.body: Equifax, S&P Global, and Fair Isaac each gained more than 5% in the final session before the holiday, an unusually tight cluster of strength within a single sub-sector. The moves lifted Financial-Data into the top performers of the day and raise the question of whether a durable rotation into data-analytics business models is underway.

briefs.headline: NATO Gathers in Ankara With Alliance Cohesion in Focus
briefs.body: Allied leaders, including President Trump, are convening in Ankara for a summit expected to reaffirm collective-defence commitments. Draft summit language circulating ahead of the meeting describes the alliance's mutual-defence pledge as ironclad, though underlying tensions over burden-sharing and strategic priorities remain a backdrop investors will monitor for market-moving statements.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Missile Strike Raises Tanker and Insurance Alarm
briefs.body: A container ship struck by a missile in the Strait of Hormuz may be headed for scrapping, according to senior shipping-company commentary. The incident adds a tangible supply-chain risk dimension to already elevated geopolitical anxiety, potentially affecting freight rates and energy-transit insurance premiums in the days ahead.

briefs.headline: Gulf Crude Flows Hit Record as Iran Tension Mounts
briefs.body: Gulf oil exports rose sharply in June, driven by record outflows from the UAE, even as the Islamic Republic's political future remains uncertain following the Supreme Leader's death. Reports of Iran exploring new crude sales to Asian buyers, alongside comparisons to the 1979 oil shock, underscore the degree of uncertainty now embedded in energy markets heading into the new week.

briefs.headline: Healthcare and Consumer Resilience Offers Bulls a Foothold
briefs.body: With Healthcare advancing 3.2% and Consumer names up 1.9% on the final pre-holiday session, defensive and spending-driven sectors provided a counterweight to the semiconductor rout. Breadth finished at 109 advancers versus 89 decliners across the universe, a modestly positive read that may encourage dip-buyers when the shortened trading week opens.
